prompt:
Passage 1
A regional supply company recently changed how it organizes replacement parts in its main storage room. Previously, parts were grouped mostly by supplier name. This made sense to the purchasing department, but it often slowed down employees who needed to find parts quickly for repair jobs.
After reviewing usage records, managers reorganized the storage room by part type and frequency of use. Commonly used items were moved closer to the service counter, while less frequently used parts were placed in clearly labeled secondary storage areas.
Supervisors report that employees now spend less time searching for parts. The company also plans to review the layout every six months to make sure the storage system continues to match actual work needs.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: The company stopped buying replacement parts.
choices.B: A storage-room reorganization helped employees locate parts more efficiently.
choices.C: Suppliers now decide where parts are stored.
choices.D: Employees no longer need replacement parts for repairs.

explanation: The passage explains how reorganizing the storage room by part type and usage helped employees find parts more efficiently. The other choices describe actions not stated in the passage.

prompt:
Passage 2
MEMO
To: All Production Employees
Subject: Updated Breakroom Cleaning Procedure
Beginning March 4, employees using the breakroom must place food waste in the marked containers and wipe tables after use. Cleaning supplies will be available near the sink.
The updated procedure was created after several employees reported spills, food scraps, and overflowing trash containers at the end of shifts. While the cleaning crew will continue to perform regular deep cleaning, employees are expected to help keep the shared space usable throughout the workday.
Supervisors will review the procedure during team meetings this week.

What is the purpose of the memo?

choices.A: To announce a new production schedule
choices.B: To explain an updated breakroom cleaning procedure
choices.C: To cancel employee break periods

explanation: The memo announces and explains the updated breakroom cleaning procedure for production employees.

prompt:
Passage 3
Many companies use onboarding programs to help new employees begin work successfully. An onboarding program may include paperwork, introductions to coworkers, safety training, job-shadowing, and explanations of company policies.
A strong onboarding process can help new employees understand expectations early. It can also reduce confusion by showing workers where to find information and whom to contact when questions arise. Without clear guidance, new employees may take longer to become comfortable and productive.
Onboarding is not limited to the first day of work. Some organizations continue the process over several weeks or months, especially for jobs that involve multiple systems, safety procedures, or specialized tasks.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: New employees should learn everything without assistance.
choices.B: Onboarding programs help new employees understand their roles and become productive.
choices.C: Company policies are useful only for managers.
choices.D: Job-shadowing should replace all safety training.

explanation: The passage focuses on how onboarding helps new employees understand expectations, reduce confusion, and become productive.

prompt:
Passage 4
Before electronic timekeeping systems became common, many workplaces used paper time cards. Employees recorded their arrival and departure times by writing them down or stamping a card in a mechanical clock. Payroll staff later reviewed the cards and calculated hours worked.
Electronic timekeeping systems changed this process by allowing employees to clock in and out using badges, computers, or mobile devices. These systems can reduce calculation errors and make payroll information available more quickly. They may also help supervisors identify attendance patterns.
However, electronic systems still require clear procedures. Employees need to know when to clock in, how to report missed punches, and whom to contact if there is a system problem.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Paper time cards are more advanced than electronic systems.
choices.B: Payroll staff no longer review any records.
choices.C: Electronic timekeeping improved payroll tracking but still requires clear procedures.
choices.D: Employees should not record work hours.

explanation: The passage explains that electronic timekeeping improves payroll tracking but still needs clear procedures for employees.

prompt:
Passage 5
A small manufacturing company recently began cross-training employees in several production areas. Before the program, many employees were trained for only one main task. When someone was absent, supervisors sometimes had difficulty finding another worker who could cover the position.
Cross-training allows employees to learn additional tasks outside their usual assignment. The company began with short training sessions led by experienced workers. Employees practiced under supervision before being approved to work independently in a second area.
Managers believe the program will improve scheduling flexibility and reduce delays caused by absences. Some employees also said the training helped them better understand how their work connects to the rest of the production process.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Employees should perform only one task during their careers.
choices.B: The company reduced training to save time.
choices.C: Cross-training helped the company improve flexibility and employee understanding.

explanation: The passage explains how cross-training improves scheduling flexibility and helps employees understand the production process better.

prompt:
Passage 6
Many public buildings use automatic doors to improve access for visitors. These doors can be especially helpful for people carrying packages, pushing carts, using wheelchairs, or entering with young children.
Automatic doors rely on sensors that detect movement or presence near the entrance. When the system works properly, the door opens without the visitor needing to pull a handle. This can make entry easier and reduce crowding near busy entrances.
Like other building systems, automatic doors require maintenance. Sensors may need adjustment, tracks must be kept clear, and mechanical parts may require inspection. Regular checks help ensure the doors operate safely and consistently.

What is the passage mainly about?

choices.A: Why automatic doors should be removed from public buildings
choices.B: How automatic doors improve access and require maintenance
choices.C: Why visitors should avoid busy entrances

explanation: The passage describes how automatic doors improve access and explains why they need regular maintenance.

prompt:
Passage 7
Many cities operate snow removal programs during winter weather. Crews may spread salt or sand, plow major roads, clear public parking areas, and respond to hazardous conditions. The order in which streets are cleared often depends on traffic volume, emergency access needs, and available equipment.
Main roads and routes near hospitals, fire stations, and police stations are usually given high priority. Residential streets may be cleared later, especially during storms that continue for several hours. This does not mean those streets are ignored; rather, crews must focus first on areas that affect the greatest number of people or are essential for emergency response.
City officials often remind residents to move parked vehicles when possible so plows can clear streets more effectively.

What is the main idea of the passage?

choices.A: Snow removal programs prioritize streets based on safety and access needs.
choices.B: Residential streets are never cleared after snowstorms.
choices.C: Cities remove snow only from parking lots.
choices.D: Salt and sand are no longer used in winter.

explanation: The passage explains how snow removal programs prioritize roads based on traffic, safety, emergency access, and available equipment.

prompt:
Passage 8
A regional employer recently reviewed its apprenticeship application workshops. These workshops help applicants understand the application process, gather required documents, and practice basic reading and math skills.
Staff found that applicants who attended a workshop were more likely to submit complete applications. Many participants said the workshop helped them understand deadlines and avoid missing required forms. However, staff also noticed that some applicants still struggled with providing accurate contact information.
In response, the employer revised the workshop checklist to include a separate step for confirming phone numbers, email addresses, and mailing addresses. Staff believe this small change may reduce delays in communicating with applicants.

What is the main purpose of the workshops?

choices.A: To replace apprenticeship training
choices.B: To help applicants understand the application process and prepare materials
choices.C: To guarantee every applicant a job

explanation: The workshops help applicants understand the process, gather documents, and practice basic reading and math skills.

explanation: The revised checklist adds a step for confirming contact information, which may reduce delays in communication.
